caveats: a: No public wage-weighted task inventory specific to malt manufacturing was found.
a: The Thermo Fisher paper is vendor-affiliated and describes laboratory automation, not the whole plant.
rho: This is implementation judgment, not a measured adoption rate.
rho: Large modern plants and small craft maltsters may have very different control-system maturity.
e: The frozen dataset estimates zero firms in the $1-10M EBITDA band, so this conditional eligibility share does not imply an available target count.
e: Public establishment counts do not reveal firm ownership, profitability, or captive status.
s5: Gallup covers all U.S. employer businesses, not malt manufacturing or the LMM EBITDA band.
s5: Intentions may not become completed transfers and include gifts or rare public offerings.
q: No public contract sample or post-productivity pricing study specific to U.S. maltsters was located.
q: Craft-brewery conditions are only part of the customer base and may overstate pricing pressure for distilling or food customers.
d5: Barley FAI includes uses other than malt and is not identical to merchant malt shipments.
d5: Craft beer is a subset of beer demand and uses malt at a different intensity from large-scale brewing.
o: This is a structural judgment rather than a measured five-year retention share.
o: The construct separates operator requirement from declining end-market volume, which is captured in d5.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Recurring business-to-business sales of specification-grade malt, typically by shipment or supply agreement, with revenue tied to physical volume and negotiated product specifications.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive malt operations inside breweries, brewing itself, malt extracts and syrups, shell entities, and businesses without transferable operating assets or external customers.
lens.included_activities: Independent malt houses manufacturing barley, rye, or other-grain malt for repeat sale to external breweries, distillers, and food customers.

a.bridge: O*NET and BLS describe food batchmakers and food-processing equipment workers rather than the wage-weighted 311213 workforce. I map recordkeeping, monitoring, recipe control, scheduling, and inspection to AI-assistable work, while treating cleaning, material handling, equipment tending, and sensory or safety-critical decisions as weakly substitutable. This likely overstates remaining opportunity in large malt houses because malt testing and process control are already substantially automated.
caveats: No public wage-weighted task inventory specific to malt manufacturing was found.
The Thermo Fisher paper is vendor-affiliated and describes laboratory automation, not the whole plant.
a.change_evidence: A 311213-specific OEWS staffing table combined with time-and-motion interviews across production, laboratory, maintenance, logistics, sales, and administrative roles, explicitly identifying already automated tasks.
a.rationale: The relevant production proxy mixes information-heavy tasks such as recording batch data and monitoring instruments with substantial physical equipment operation, sanitation, loading, and quality work. Existing malt-lab automation further reduces the not-yet-automated base, leaving a bounded opportunity in documentation, exception detection, planning, maintenance triage, and customer/specification administration.

d5.bridge: USDA reports U.S. beer production down more than 13% from 2016/17 to 2023/24 and barley food/alcohol/industrial use forecast at 110.7 million bushels in 2024/25, 27% below 2016/17; the Brewers Association reports 2024 craft volume down 3.9% and overall beer volume down 1.2%. I translate these downstream and input-market trends into a five-year constant-quality demand ratio for U.S. merchant malt, allowing a less severe decline in the high case because distilling, food, specialty, exports, and share shifts can offset beer weakness. The proxies may overstate decline if merchant malt gains share from captive or imported supply, and understate it if beer contraction accelerates.
caveats: Barley FAI includes uses other than malt and is not identical to merchant malt shipments.
Craft beer is a subset of beer demand and uses malt at a different intensity from large-scale brewing.
d5.change_evidence: USDA malt production and disappearance by end use, TTB beer production through 2031, maltster shipment volumes split among brewing, distilling, food, export, and specialty applications, and customer forecasts under constant specifications.
d5.rationale: Brewing is the primary demand channel and its long decline continued in recent data. Specialty uses and non-beer customers provide some diversification, but the central case remains a material real-volume contraction rather than stable demand.

caveats: The frozen dataset estimates zero firms in the $1-10M EBITDA band, so this conditional eligibility share does not imply an available target count.
Public establishment counts do not reveal firm ownership, profitability, or captive status.
e.change_evidence: A complete establishment-to-firm map for NAICS 311213 with ownership, captive versus merchant status, revenue, normalized EBITDA, customer concentration, environmental liabilities, and owner dependence.
e.rationale: A maltster with external repeat customers, separable plant assets, transferable permits and quality systems, and no dependence on a single owner can fit the lens. Eligibility is reduced by captive or vertically integrated operations, customer concentration, commodity exposure, and facilities whose economics or environmental liabilities do not support a standalone control acquisition.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 311213 ($98,586,564) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 311213 ($1,027,076,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1525 (margins.json[31]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Food Processing EBITDA/Sales 15.25% (dominant subsector of NAICS 31)); revenue/employee $1,269,562 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 311213); class <5: 12 firms, avg revenue/firm $385,417 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $58,776 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 5 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,677,200 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $255,773 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 3 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,504,000 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $381,860 -> out of $1-10M band; class 100-499: 3 firms, avg revenue/firm $200,784,000 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $30,619,560 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 0

caveats: This is a structural judgment rather than a measured five-year retention share.
The construct separates operator requirement from declining end-market volume, which is captured in d5.
o.change_evidence: Customer make-versus-buy surveys, announced captive malting capacity, import-share forecasts, and brewer formulation studies measuring substitution away from externally supplied malt.
o.rationale: The service basket is physical grain conversion to specification-grade malt; software cannot steep, germinate, kiln, test, store, and ship the product. AI may reduce staffing but does not remove accountable plant operation. The residual loss reflects possible brewery self-supply, vertical integration, imports, or substitution toward extracts and other inputs.

caveats: No public contract sample or post-productivity pricing study specific to U.S. maltsters was located.
Craft-brewery conditions are only part of the customer base and may overstate pricing pressure for distilling or food customers.
q.change_evidence: Customer contracts and invoice cohorts showing indexation, cost-plus clauses, renewal repricing, gross margin by malt type, customer concentration, bid frequency, and realized price changes following documented productivity improvements.
q.rationale: Malt is a recurring physical input sold to sophisticated breweries and distillers. An operator can initially retain savings through better yield, scheduling, quality consistency, and lower overhead, but renewals, competing suppliers, raw-material formulas, and financially pressured brewery customers should pass through a meaningful share. Retention should be stronger for differentiated specialty malt and weaker for commodity base malt.

caveats: This is implementation judgment, not a measured adoption rate.
Large modern plants and small craft maltsters may have very different control-system maturity.
rho.change_evidence: Plant-level implementation records from maltsters showing baseline hours, validated use cases, adoption dates, false-alarm rates, quality deviations, and headcount or hiring effects over at least four production cycles.
rho.rationale: Over five years, AI copilots and anomaly-detection tools can plausibly enter documentation, scheduling, quality review, and maintenance workflows, but food-safety accountability, validation against standard methods, integration with plant controls, small-industry scale, and limited tolerance for batch failures constrain implementation. The midpoint assumes augmentation and avoided hiring more often than unattended operation.

s5.bridge: Gallup measured plans among U.S. business owners generally: 22% of employer-business owners planned to sell or transfer ownership within five years. I use that as the center, trim slightly for a capital-intensive, highly concentrated manufacturing niche where family transfers, strategic ownership, and non-qualifying transactions may not create an acquirable control opportunity. The broad survey could overstate qualifying maltster transfers, while unplanned sales could make it understate realized transfers.
caveats: Gallup covers all U.S. employer businesses, not malt manufacturing or the LMM EBITDA band.
Intentions may not become completed transfers and include gifts or rare public offerings.
s5.change_evidence: Five years of malt-manufacturing control transactions from PitchBook, Capital IQ, GF Data, and state business registries, paired with owner-age and succession interviews for every independent U.S. maltster.
s5.rationale: The employer-business survey provides a direct five-year ownership-plan anchor but not an industry-specific realized transaction rate. A wide range reflects both the tiny industry population and uncertainty about whether planned transfers are external, controlling, and operationally transferable.

principal_driver: Recurring need for accountable physical conversion and quality assurance keeps most surviving malt demand operator-required.
principal_weakness: The frozen estimate of zero LMM-band firms combines with declining brewing demand to make target availability exceptionally weak.
